summary: The Federal Transit Administration launches an investigation into MARTA following two stabbing attacks, including one fatal, that have shaken public confidence ahead of the World Cup. Old Fourth Ward prepares to host a three-day soccer kickoff festival starting June 11. The State Election Board passes a non-binding resolution allowing counties to switch to hand-marked ballots. Black women-owned businesses open at the Atlanta Municipal Market. And the city orders an independent investigation into the Chattahoochee River fish kill that saw thousands of fish die from sewage overflow.

topStory.headline: Federal Transit Administration Launches Investigation into MARTA Safety
topStory.summary: U.S. Transportation Secretary Sean Duffy directs the FTA to examine MARTA's security spending, safety protocols, and risks to riders following two violent attacks in late May that left one passenger dead.
topStory.body: The Federal Transit Administration will launch an investigation into MARTA after two stabbing attacks frightened commuters last month. U.S. Transportation Secretary Sean Duffy announced Thursday that his agency will look into MARTA's security spending, safety protocols, and risks to riders and workers. "Every American should be disturbed by the horrific crimes we have seen on MARTA in the last month," Duffy said in a statement. "No one should be forced to fear for their safety simply because they choose to ride public transit." On May 30, 66-year-old Margaret Sams-Swan died after being stabbed multiple times on a MARTA train at the Oakland City Station. A 25-year-old Decatur man is in custody and faces federal charges related to the killing. On May 24, a 40-year-old man was stabbed at the Georgia State MARTA station following an altercation. Police are seeking a suspect in that attack. The transit agency serves more than 500,000 people every weekday across trains, buses, and streetcars. It's likely to come under increased scrutiny when soccer fans descend on the city for World Cup matches starting June 15. Duffy says MARTA has 15 days to submit data on security including safety funding and action plans. In a statement, MARTA said it welcomes the opportunity to work with the FTA.

stories.headline: Old Fourth Ward Soccer Kickoff Festival Offers World Cup Celebration Beyond the Stadium
stories.summary: The O4W Neighborhood Association will transform Historic Fourth Ward Park into a free, public gathering space for fans to celebrate the World Cup opening June 11-13, whether or not they have match tickets.
stories.body: As Atlanta prepares to welcome the world for the World Cup, residents won't need a match ticket to join the excitement. The Old Fourth Ward Neighborhood Association will host the O4W Soccer Kickoff Festival June 11-13, transforming Historic Fourth Ward Park and surrounding neighborhood destinations into gathering spaces where residents, visitors, and soccer fans can celebrate together. All festival events are free and open to the public. "The World Cup shouldn't be something Atlantans only watch on TV; it should be something they can walk to," said Lamar Stewart, interim executive director of Showcase Atlanta. "Whether you have a ticket to a match or not, there will be a place for you to show up, celebrate, and feel like this moment belongs to you." The celebration begins Thursday, June 11, with the O4W Bar Crawl starting at 5 p.m. along the Eastside BeltLine and Edgewood Avenue. Participating establishments will offer specialty food, drinks, and experiences inspired by World Cup countries. Additional programming throughout the weekend includes watch parties, DJ sets, and neighborhood showcases.
stories.imageAlt: Historic Fourth Ward Park during festival

stories.headline: Where to Watch the World Cup Around Atlanta
stories.summary: From soccer bars like Brewhouse Cafe's new South Downtown location to the 34-day Decatur WatchFest with concerts by Big Boi and Indigo Girls, here's your guide to experiencing the tournament across the city.
stories.body: Atlanta's pubs are prepped to welcome a summer of world-class soccer in their own backyard. The Brewhouse Cafe, a staple in Little Five Points, opened a second location in South Downtown in May. The new spot will host live DJs and team-themed days while showing every World Cup game. Downtown, Der Biergarten offers German-themed viewing, while Manny's in Grant Park and Fado Irish Pub locations in Buckhead and Midtown provide options for avoiding the thickest crowds. A few MARTA stops east of downtown, the Decatur WatchFest runs for 34 days as a local hub for the area's soccer fans. Neighborhood bars and restaurants will broadcast matches, with DJs and games set up in Decatur Square. The city is also investing heavily in concerts: Big Boi performs June 11 and the Indigo Girls on July 19, with free tickets available in advance. "This is a once-in-a-lifetime opportunity," festival planners said.
stories.imageAlt: Brewhouse Cafe soccer fans watching match

stories.headline: Black Women-Owned Businesses Open Inside Atlanta Municipal Market
stories.summary: Four new businesses have opened in the historic Sweet Auburn market, including A Taste of Secrets (A.T.O.S.), a Jamaican-Puerto Rican fusion restaurant opening June 13.
stories.body: The lights were low and the energy was high inside the Atlanta Municipal Market, a historic flea market on Edgewood Avenue in the heart of the Sweet Auburn District. Four new businesses have opened in the market, many owned by Black women. A ribbon-cutting ceremony, food-tasting, and mini-tour took place Friday morning. "God is right on time," said Mychel Rodriguez, mother of Niddy Rodriguez, co-owner of A Taste of Secrets (A.T.O.S.), a Jamaican-Puerto Rican fusion restaurant scheduled to open June 13. Chefs Niddy Rodriguez and Miish Hudson are the team behind A.T.O.S. Both have experience as executive chefs. "There were so many reasons," Rodriguez said of opening in the market. "This Atlanta Municipal Market is a hub for Atlantans." "This is a historical building," added Hudson. City leaders say the World Cup could generate up to $1 billion in economic impact for Atlanta, and merchants at the market hope to welcome visitors from around the world. Other new businesses include Dolce Tropical, owned by Latasha Smith and her daughter Jordan Smith.
stories.imageAlt: Business owners at Atlanta Municipal Market ribbon cutting

stories.headline: Sankranti Indian Kitchen Planning Buckhead Expansion
stories.summary: The fast-casual Indian restaurant chain is opening a new location at 3420 Piedmont Road with seating for 43 plus outdoor dining, following closures of its Dunwoody and Decatur locations.
stories.body: Indian restaurant Sankranti will be opening a new restaurant in Buckhead, according to a recent permit filing. It is slated to open in a 1,560-square-foot ground-floor space at 3420 Piedmont Rd NE. The permit indicates that construction is still forthcoming in the space. When completed, the restaurant will seat 43 people and will also feature outdoor dining. Sankranti has one existing location in Georgia Tech's Technology Research Building that opened in December 2023. The brand previously had locations in Dunwoody and Decatur, but both shuttered late last year. The brand also has a downtown location listed as "coming soon" at 27 Piedmont Ave. NE, though an exact timeline has not been announced. The restaurant serves a fast-casual Indian menu featuring curry bowls, biryani, samosas, chaat, and naan.

stories.headline: State Election Board Passes Resolution Allowing Counties to Switch to Hand-Marked Ballots
stories.summary: The non-binding 3-1 vote contradicts Secretary of State guidance and comes ahead of a special legislative session on June 17 to address ballot QR codes before the July 1 deadline.
stories.body:
Georgia's State Election Board has advanced a resolution seeking to implement hand-marked paper ballots ahead of the November 2026 general election, contradicting guidelines from the secretary of state's office directing counties to continue using Georgia's current voting machines.

The resolution, which is not binding, was introduced by newly appointed Vice Chair Janelle King and passed in a 3-1 vote Thursday.

King said the resolution would allow county election workers to switch to hand-marked paper ballots if the state Legislature fails to address an upcoming deadline. Under Senate Bill 189, which passed in 2024, QR codes cannot be used for the official ballot count after July 1.

A special session to address ballot QR codes, redistricting, and local property tax referendums is set to begin on June 17. State lawmakers, not the board, are responsible for establishing Georgia's election laws.

Board member Janice Johnston announced her resignation from the board Thursday.

Multiple board members have raised concerns that state lawmakers may not resolve the QR code issue in time for counties to prepare.

stories.headline: City Opens Independent Investigation into Chattahoochee River Fish Die-Off
stories.summary: Atlanta's Department of Watershed Management has retained Brown and Caldwell to evaluate the West Area CSO Tunnel Treatment Facility after sewage overflow caused one of the worst fish die-offs in years.
stories.body: The city of Atlanta is opening an independent investigation after thousands of fish were found dead in the Chattahoochee River. The Atlanta Department of Watershed Management said it has "retained Brown and Caldwell, an engineering and consulting firm, to conduct an independent evaluation of system operations at the West Area CSO Tunnel Treatment Facility and the associated tunnel operations." The investigation follows a fish kill last month that the Chattahoochee Riverkeeper called one of the worst in years. For 20 miles downstream of the Chattahoochee River National Recreation Area, the riverkeeper found banks coated in black muck and thousands of dead fish. "I've never seen anything this devastating on any water system," said Jason Ulseth of the Chattahoochee Riverkeeper. During flash flooding last month, sewage overflowed into the river, causing oxygen levels in the water to drop. Because of that, fish in that stretch of the Chattahoochee suffocated. The city says the independent review will assess rainfall conditions, system performance during the event, and the source of reported sludge residue.
stories.imageAlt: Dead fish along Chattahoochee River bank

stories.headline: After BeltLine Tragedy, Atlanta's Diversion Center Faces Scrutiny
stories.summary: The man accused in the fatal attack on Alyssa Paige had been taken to the Center for Diversion and Services just 12 days earlier after allegedly threatening someone with a knife downtown.
stories.body: Less than two weeks before Jahmare Brown was arrested in the fatal stabbing of 23-year-old Alyssa Paige on the BeltLine Trail, he had been accused of waving a knife and threatening to kill a man in Downtown Atlanta. According to records uncovered by WSB-TV, Brown, 21, was not arrested. Instead, the responding Atlanta Police officer and the person who reported the incident agreed Brown should be taken to the Center for Diversion and Services to receive mental health treatment. The Diversion Center is a joint venture between Atlanta, Fulton County, and Grady Hospital, with Grady operating the center. People brought there can receive a hot meal, a shower, and free laundry before being referred to legal services and case managers for housing, food stamps, mental health treatment, and substance use programs. Since opening in 2025, the $3 million facility on Peachtree Street has been largely underused by local law enforcement. At its peak, the center averaged eight daily referrals, well below the 40 it was designed to handle. The Police Alternatives and Diversion Initiative, which had been providing long-term case management at the center, ceased operations earlier this year.

stories.headline: Human Rights Group Warns More Inmates Will Die at Fulton County Jail
stories.summary: The Southern Center for Human Rights says horrifying accounts from former prisoners expose ongoing crumbling conditions, including inadequate food, water, and sleep accommodations.
stories.body:
An organization advocating for decency and justice believes more inmates will die at the Fulton County Jail after listening to the horrifying accounts of two former prisoners.

Earlier this week, former inmates Connie Lott and Amber Tyer exposed crumbling conditions, revealing they went without adequate food, water, or a decent place to sleep.

"Once you get in there, it's like I would rather die than just sit here," Lott said.

Attorney Devin Franklin with the Southern Center for Human Rights said it was tough to listen to their stories. He warned that the exact same conditions the women articulated have already led to dozens of past deaths inside the jail.

Franklin believes leadership is a big part of the problem and notes many complaints could be solved with common courtesy and basic human dignity. He points out that the ongoing issues have nothing to do with the physical, crumbling walls of the outdated building.

"Regardless of what a person is charged with, of how long they're in custody, they should always be treated with the basic human dignity," Franklin said.

Sheriff Patrick Labat has not yet publicly responded to the specific allegations.

stories.headline: Ireland Shows How Sports Can Build Business Ties Ahead of World Cup
stories.summary: Irish officials in Atlanta highlight sports as an emerging area of diplomacy, using the World Cup as an opportunity to strengthen economic connections with Georgia.
stories.body:
"For Ireland, sport is an emerging and very exciting area of diplomacy in an increasingly complex world."

That message came from Irish officials gathered in Atlanta ahead of the FIFA World Cup, highlighting how major sporting events create opportunities for international business development.

Ireland's presence in Atlanta for the World Cup represents a strategic effort to strengthen economic ties with Georgia. The state has long been a destination for Irish investment, with numerous Irish companies maintaining operations in metro Atlanta.

The World Cup, which brings matches to Mercedes-Benz Stadium starting June 15, provides a unique platform for nations to showcase their cultures and build relationships beyond the pitch.

Irish representatives emphasized the importance of face-to-face connections during global events, noting that sports create neutral ground for business conversations that might not happen in traditional settings.

The initiative reflects a broader trend of nations using major sporting events as soft power tools to advance diplomatic and economic goals.

stories.headline: Atlanta's GenAI Healthcare Launches NxRing Smart Health Wearable
stories.summary: The Georgia Tech-born startup is now taking pre-orders for its health ring that translates real-time biometrics and biomarkers, building on its NexCura wellness platform.
stories.body: When Hypepotamus first covered a scrappy Georgia Tech startup pitching health solutions in 2024, the team was one to watch at the Atlanta Startup Awards ceremony. Now GenAI Healthcare is getting ready to put a wearable health device in customers' hands. Its product, NxRing, is now available for pre-orders. The device translates real-time biometrics and biomarkers, building on the company's NexCura platform for wellness insights. "For manufacturing, we wanted to ensure the highest quality hardware for our customers," said CEO Ramesh Sundararajan. "It was difficult to find a unit that would realize our vision of the ring. We landed in Japan." The company is also rolling out a new Physical Intelligence Lab to advance its capabilities. "In building NexCura, our primary goal was always to provide a platform that would enable individuals to take ownership of their health and to stop reacting to health concerns, but preventing them in the first place," Sundararajan said. GenAI Healthcare is based in Midtown, joining Atlanta's growing health tech ecosystem.

stories.headline: Democrats Invested Millions to Challenge Supreme Court Justices. Is It a Sign of What's to Come?
stories.summary: Despite $8 million in spending and record Democratic early voter turnout, both incumbent state Supreme Court justices held their seats in the May nonpartisan election.
stories.body: Georgia Democrats had high hopes of tipping the balance on the state's highest court in February, when two candidates announced challenges to sitting justices during the May nonpartisan elections. But despite $8 million in spending from the Democratic Party of Georgia and record early voter turnout where Democratic voters surpassed Republicans, both incumbent justices held their seats. Presiding Justice Sarah Warren received nearly 60% of the vote, according to unofficial results from the secretary of state's office, while Justice Charlie Bethel finished with roughly 51%. Former Democratic state Sen. Jen Jordan challenged Warren, while attorney Miracle Rankin ran against Bethel. The results raise questions about the effectiveness of investing heavily in down-ballot judicial races and whether such efforts can succeed even with favorable turnout conditions. Jordan, who previously represented parts of Buckhead and Sandy Springs in the state Senate, ran on reproductive rights and democracy issues.

stories.headline: I-285 Closes Again This Weekend for West Side Reconstruction
stories.summary: The $206 million project will shut down all lanes between MLK Drive and Cascade Road from 7 p.m. Friday through 5 a.m. Monday as crews rebuild one of metro Atlanta's worst highway stretches.
stories.body:
The Georgia Department of Transportation will once again close a portion of I-285 on the west side for weekend road work.

The full closure in both directions will be located between State Route 139/Martin Luther King, Jr. Drive (exit 9) and Cascade Road (exit 7) in Fulton County.

The closure begins at 7 p.m. Friday, June 5, and will remain in place until 5 a.m. Monday, June 8.

Motorists can expect delays and congestion on alternate routes, including I-20, I-75/I-85, and Langford Parkway.

Southbound I-285 traffic will be diverted to I-20 (Exit 10 A) to I-75/I-85 southbound. Northbound and westbound I-285 traffic will be diverted to SR 166/Langford Parkway (Exit 5).

This $206 million effort is one of two major projects to repair and replace concrete slabs along the westside Perimeter corridor. Construction is scheduled for completion in 2028.

The project represents a full-depth reconstruction rather than basic resurfacing of 10 miles of highway that officials consider one of the worst stretches in the metro area.
